title: Synapse — Neural Network Lab
tagline: Interactive playground that implements backpropagation from scratch in TypeScript — watch neural networks learn decision boundaries in real-time.
details: A neural network laboratory built entirely from scratch — no TensorFlow, no PyTorch, just raw TypeScript implementing forward propagation, backpropagation, and gradient descent. Users configure network architecture (layers, neurons, activation functions), pick a dataset, and watch the decision boundary evolve in real-time as the network trains.
highlights: Full neural network engine from scratch: forward pass, backpropagation, and SGD — zero ML library dependencies
Real-time decision boundary visualization renders 6,400 predictions per frame on an 80×80 Canvas grid
He weight initialization (sqrt(2/fan_in)) for stable gradient flow with ReLU networks
requestAnimationFrame training loop runs 5 epochs/frame while maintaining 60fps UI responsiveness
5 procedural datasets (XOR, Spiral, Moons, Circle, Gaussian) with configurable architecture up to 5 hidden layers
learnings: Building backpropagation from scratch forces you to understand every derivative in the chain rule — there's no loss.backward() to hide behind. The hardest part wasn't the math; it was debugging vanishing gradients and realizing the sigmoid output layer needed separate derivative handling from hidden layers.

title: F1 AI Race Engineer
tagline: End-to-end AI system that replays historical F1 races with a learned pit-strategy model and live LLM commentary.
details: An AI 'race engineer' that replays any historical Grand Prix (2018+) and does what real F1 teams do in real time: predicts tyre degradation, recommends pit-stop windows, and narrates the race in the voice of Sky Sports' David Croft. The whole pipeline pre-renders to a 100KB JSON file so the site runs static on GitHub Pages with zero backend cost.
highlights: Trained XGBoost tyre-degradation model on 11,000+ cleaned laps across 12 races (0.485s MAE on held-out test set)
Monte Carlo strategy engine enumerates ~500 pit-stop combinations and recommends the optimal window — matched Mercedes' real Hamilton call at 2024 Silverstone within 3 laps
Engineered ~100x speedup by batching XGBoost inference (35,000 single-row calls → ~500 batched calls)
Llama 3.3 70B via Groq generates broadcast-style commentary on detected events (pit stops, fastest laps, overtakes)
Honest debugging: discovered non-monotonic tyre curves were a visualization bug, not a model bug — held lap_number constant to isolate pure tyre-age effect
learnings: An 'AI' recommendation is just search + a learned cost function + honest evaluation. The hard part isn't the model — it's the data cleaning (fuel-correcting lap times, dropping Safety Car laps), the perf optimization (batching predictions), and being honest about what your metrics actually mean.

title: Smart Crash Detection & Traffic Management
tagline: Hardware-software integrated system that detects vehicle crashes and traffic congestion in real time using IoT sensors.
details: Designed and built a smart traffic safety system bridging hardware and software — ultrasonic sensors measure distance, vibration/impact sensors detect collisions, and IR sensors monitor road conditions. A Python layer continuously analyses the incoming sensor data, identifies abnormal patterns indicating crashes or heavy congestion, and triggers instant alerts to enable proactive accident response.
highlights: Multi-sensor fusion: ultrasonic (distance), vibration/impact (crash), and IR (road condition) sensors working in parallel
Real-time pattern detection logic identifies sudden collisions and congestion spikes from live sensor streams
Alert system fires instant notifications on crash or heavy-traffic detection for proactive response
Demonstrated via physical model — showcasing applicability in smart cities and intelligent transport systems
learnings: Working with hardware taught me that real-world data is noisy by default. Vibration sensors pick up road bumps, not just crashes — threshold tuning and debounce logic ended up being as important as the detection algorithm itself.

title: Stress Score Analysis System
tagline: NLP system that evaluates a person's stress level from written daily reflections and generates a quantitative stress score.
details: A Python-based NLP project that takes someone's written daily reflection as input and outputs a quantitative stress score. Uses keyword and phrase matching against a predefined stress-related lexicon to identify emotional patterns in text. Built with real-world application in mind — educational institutions could use it to proactively identify and support students facing mental stress.
highlights: Keyword and phrase matching against a curated stress-related lexicon to surface emotional patterns
Generates a numeric stress score usable by institutions to flag at-risk students
Applied ethical AI thinking — built with student wellbeing and mental health use cases front of mind
Demonstrates foundational NLP: tokenisation, text preprocessing, and pattern-based text analytics
learnings: NLP doesn't always need a neural network. A well-designed lexicon with smart phrase matching can be surprisingly effective — and far more explainable to the people who'd actually use the score.

title: Animal Detection & Identification System
tagline: Python computer vision system that identifies animal species from live camera feeds, video files, and static images.
details: A computer vision system capable of classifying and identifying animal species from three input modes: live camera feed, video files, and static images. Built with OpenCV and deep learning-based detection, it was designed with real-world deployment contexts in mind — zoos, wildlife sanctuaries, and anti-poaching efforts where instant detection and alerting when animals enter restricted areas could make a real difference.
highlights: Handles three input modes: live camera feed, video files, and static images — same detection pipeline for all three
Deep learning classification to identify and distinguish between different animal species
Anti-poaching use case: instant alert when an animal is detected in a restricted or protected zone
Explored real-world applications in biodiversity monitoring and wildlife conservation
learnings: Computer vision models are only as good as their training distribution. Testing on live camera feeds exposed lighting and angle variations the static image dataset didn't cover — taught me to think about deployment context from day one.

title: Object-Oriented Ticket Booking System
tagline: Console-based ticket booking system built from scratch in Python using core OOP principles.
details: A fully console-driven ticket booking application built to demonstrate solid object-oriented design. Covers user account creation and authentication, real-time ticket availability tracking, overbooking prevention, and a modular code structure designed for maintainability. Built as a ground-up exercise in applying encapsulation, classes, objects, and data abstraction to a real-world system.
highlights: User authentication system — secure account creation with username/password management
Real-time availability tracking with overbooking prevention logic
Modular OOP architecture using encapsulation, inheritance, and data abstraction
File handling for persistent data storage across sessions
learnings: Building something 'simple' from scratch forces you to make every design decision yourself — I learned more about why OOP patterns exist by implementing them than by reading about them.

title: ClaimStream
tagline: Web application for insurance claim management — built as a full-stack project with a clean UI.
details: ClaimStream is a web-based insurance claims management system. Built with vanilla HTML/CSS/JS, it demonstrates front-end architecture and UI design for a real-world use case — managing and tracking insurance claims through a clean dashboard interface.
highlights: Clean dashboard UI for claim creation, tracking, and status updates
Responsive layout designed for both desktop and mobile
Structured data flow without any framework dependencies

title: DBMS Project
tagline: Full-stack database management system built with TypeScript — relational data modelling and CRUD operations.
details: A full-stack database management system project developed as part of my B.Tech curriculum. Covers relational schema design, normalisation, and TypeScript-driven CRUD operations — bridging the gap between theory and a working web interface.
highlights: Designed relational schema with ER diagrams and normalisation up to 3NF
TypeScript backend with typed query interfaces for safe DB interactions
CRUD operations with form validation and structured error handling

title: 3D Tic-Tac-Toe
tagline: 3D tic-tac-toe built with React, React Three Fiber, and react-spring — a 2D classic made genuinely spatial.
details: Took the classic tic-tac-toe and moved it into 3D space using React Three Fiber (Three.js bindings for React) and react-spring for physics-feeling animations. The game runs fully in-browser with no backend — a fun exploration of 3D rendering and interactive game-state logic in TypeScript.
highlights: 3D board rendered with React Three Fiber — full orbit-controls camera
Smooth placement animations powered by react-spring
Game logic written in typed TypeScript with win-detection across all winning lines
learnings: React Three Fiber makes WebGL approachable but the mental model shift from 2D DOM to a 3D scene graph is steep. Learned to think in world-space coordinates instead of CSS layout.
